{"step_by_step_thinking": "The query asks whether disruption of immune regulation mechanisms is associated with adverse pregnancy outcomes, including preeclampsia (PE). Multiple documents from MEDCORP support this association. Document [0] states that disruptions in immune tolerance at the maternal-fetal interface can lead to complications including preeclampsia, recurrent pregnancy loss, and infertility. Document [3] explicitly links immune disruption to the pathophysiology of preeclampsia and other complications. Document [14] notes that preeclamptic women exhibit elevated pro-inflammatory cytokines (TNF-α, IL-6, IL-8) and reduced anti-inflammatory cytokines (IL-10), indicating an immune imbalance. Document [13] states that lower levels of HLA-G, a key immune-regulatory molecule, are associated with preeclampsia and recurrent miscarriages. Document [12] explains that a shift from Th1 to Th2 immunity promotes an anti-inflammatory state, and disruption of this balance can lead to inflammation and complications like preeclampsia. Document [11] emphasizes maternal tolerance failure leading to miscarriage and preeclampsia. Document [4] links impaired Treg cell function to infertility and recurrent spontaneous abortions. These consistent references across multiple sources confirm that immune regulation disruption is directly tied to adverse outcomes like preeclampsia. Therefore, the answer is clearly 'yes'.", "answer_choice": "A"}
